title: Bag-of-Words
description:
Implement a Bag-of-Words (BoW) vectorizer.

Given a corpus, build a vocabulary and convert documents to count vectors.

**Example:**
```
corpus = ['the cat sat', 'the cat ran']
vocab = {'cat': 0, 'ran': 1, 'sat': 2, 'the': 3}
vectors = [[1,0,1,1], [1,1,0,1]]
```

Return sorted vocabulary (alphabetical) and the document-term matrix.
constraints: Return (vocab_dict, matrix) where vocab_dict maps word->index
starter_code:
import numpy as np

def bag_of_words(corpus: list[str]) -> tuple[dict, np.ndarray]:
    """Build BoW representation of a corpus.

    Args:
        corpus: List of raw text strings

    Returns:
        (vocab, matrix) where vocab maps word to column index
        and matrix has shape (n_docs, vocab_size)
    """
    pass
explanation: Bag-of-Words represents text as a vector of word counts, discarding word order and grammar. Despite its simplicity, BoW is effective for many classification tasks. The vocabulary size determines the feature dimension. Extensions include TF-IDF (down-weighting frequent words) and n-gram BoW (capturing some local order). It is the foundation for more advanced representations.

test_cases.name: Correct vocab size
test_cases.code:
import numpy as np
vocab, mat = bag_of_words(['the cat sat', 'the cat ran'])
assert len(vocab) == 4 and mat.shape == (2, 4)

test_cases.name: Counts are correct
test_cases.code:
import numpy as np
vocab, mat = bag_of_words(['a a b', 'b c'])
assert mat[0, vocab['a']] == 2 and mat[0, vocab['b']] == 1

test_cases.name: Vocab is alphabetically sorted
test_cases.code:
import numpy as np
vocab, _ = bag_of_words(['cat bat'])
words = sorted(vocab, key=vocab.get)
assert words == sorted(words)

test_cases.name: Single document
test_cases.code:
import numpy as np
vocab, mat = bag_of_words(['hello world'])
assert mat.shape == (1, 2) and mat.sum() == 2

hints: Collect all unique words across the corpus for the vocabulary
Sort vocabulary alphabetically and assign integer indices
